summary:
Miguel de Cervantes's Don Quixote (Part I: 1605, Part II: 1615 AD) is widely considered the first modern novel and one of the greatest works of fiction in any language — a Spanish literary masterpiece about an idealistic country gentleman who reads too many chivalric romances, loses his mind, and sets out to become a knight-errant, tilting at windmills he mistakes for giants, accompanied by his down-to-earth squire Sancho Panza. Cervantes wrote the book partly while in prison, partly satirizing the outdated chivalric literature that was still popular, and partly reflecting on the tension between idealism and reality, illusion and truth — themes that resonate across four centuries. The book's second part (1615) is particularly sophisticated, with characters who have read the first part and engage in complex meta-fictional games with the protagonist.

Don Quixote's influence on literature has been incalculable. It established many of the conventions of the novel as a form: unreliable narrators, complex psychological characterization, the gap between characters' perceptions and reality, and the self-referential awareness of fictionality. Nearly every major novelist has acknowledged its influence. In [[event:101|Spanish imperial culture]], the book reflected both nostalgia for and satire of the chivalric heroism that had animated the conquest era. Cervantes himself had lived the adventures he satirized — he fought at the [[event:2377|Battle of Lepanto]] (1571), where he was permanently wounded, was captured by Barbary pirates and spent five years as a slave in Algiers, and struggled in poverty throughout his later life despite Don Quixote's commercial success. The character of Don Quixote — the idealist who charges at windmills — has become one of literature's most universal archetypes.
